prompt: Which statement best reflects the consultant/nurse role in a doctor-led hair restoration pathway (Volume 1 Week 1: coordinated clinic, non-diagnostic support)?

options.label: Confirm the patient diagnosis during the first consultation
Support information gathering, education, and handover within scope
Set treatment plans independently before doctor review

modelAnswer: Support information gathering, education, and handover within scope.
rationale: Support staff structure information, educate within scope, document neutrally, and escalate; they do not diagnose or set treatment plans independently.

criteria.label: Correct answer
criteria.guidance: Select the role-safe option.

prompt: A patient asks you to confirm whether they are a good candidate and what surgery they should have before the doctor has assessed them (Volume 1 Week 1: coordinated pathway). What is the most appropriate support-role response?

options.label: Give your personal view so they feel reassured and stay engaged
Clarify that suitability and treatment choice are doctor-led; capture their goals and questions neutrally for clinical review
Say they are probably suitable if their donor looks thick to you

modelAnswer: Clarify doctor-led suitability; document goals and questions; do not substitute for clinical judgement.
rationale: First-contact excellence is preparation and honest boundaries, not closing a clinical decision.

criteria.label: Boundary-safe choice
criteria.guidance: Select the option that preserves clinician authority.

prompt: Why is hair and scalp science literacy important for consultant/nurse teams (Volume 1 Week 3), while diagnosis remains doctor-led?

options.label: It allows independent diagnosis
It supports accurate communication and handover
It replaces the need for clinician review

modelAnswer: It supports accurate communication and handover.
rationale: Literacy improves patient explanations and handover quality; it must not be used to claim diagnostic authority or replace clinician review.

criteria.label: Correct answer
criteria.guidance: Choose the communication-safe option.

prompt: Density (hairs per unit area) and hair calibre (thickness of individual hairs) are distinct. Why does that distinction matter for consultant/nurse communication (Volume 1 Week 3)?

options.label: It lets support staff decide the patient’s diagnosis from thinning alone
It helps explain visible change in patient-friendly terms while clinical interpretation stays with the doctor
It means shedding always proves active disease

modelAnswer: Separate concepts support accurate lay explanation without claiming diagnostic authority.
rationale: Literacy serves communication and handover, not independent labelling of disease.

criteria.label: Literacy used safely
criteria.guidance: Choose communication-handover purpose over diagnosis.

prompt: A scalp image or story appears atypical and the patient reports rapid change (Volume 1 Week 4: red flags and escalation). What is the safest next step for non-doctor staff?

options.label: Reassure the patient that it is likely routine
Document neutrally and escalate for clinician review
Advise a likely diagnosis based on the image

modelAnswer: Document neutrally and escalate for clinician review.
rationale: Atypical or rapidly evolving presentations require neutral documentation and clinician review—not reassurance, naming a disease, or closing the encounter as routine.

criteria.label: Safe escalation choice
criteria.guidance: The learner must choose escalation.

prompt: Which approach best fits non-doctor staff when a patient asks you to name their condition after describing gradual crown thinning (Volume 1 Week 4: descriptive literacy, no diagnostic closure)?

options.label: State a definitive disease label so the patient stops worrying
Use neutral description of what they report and what is visible, document, and route interpretation to the clinician
Dismiss the concern as cosmetic only

modelAnswer: Describe without confirming diagnosis; support handover and appropriate review.
rationale: Presentation literacy anchors education in observation, not private diagnostic authority.

criteria.label: Scope-safe description
criteria.guidance: Avoid naming disease as fact from non-doctor staff.

prompt: Write a structured first-consultation note (Volume 2 Week 5) with headings for opening context, history, visual/reported concerns, treatment history, red flags or caution points, stated goals and underlying worries, and clear doctor handover questions—neutral, factual, no diagnostic labels or good-candidate language unless policy explicitly allows.

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Presenting concern, key history points, expectations, red flags, and handover questions.

modelAnswer: Headings should mirror a professional consult arc; wording factual; emotional drivers captured; escalation or review needs explicit.
rationale: Volume 2 Week 5 treats the note as a clinical communication tool for the next clinician.

criteria.label: Documentation structure
criteria.guidance: Assess Volume 2 Week 5 completeness: structure, neutrality, goals/fears, caution flags, handover usefulness.

prompt: A patient requests immediate booking but key screening information is incomplete (Volume 2 Week 6: pause, document, escalate). What is the safest action for non-doctor staff?

options.label: Proceed and gather the rest later
Escalate and complete the missing screening record first
Assure them they are probably suitable

modelAnswer: Escalate and complete the missing screening record first.
rationale: Commercial tempo must not replace screening judgement; incomplete data requires completion and appropriate review per Volume 2 Week 6.

criteria.label: Safe triage choice
criteria.guidance: Select completion of screening and escalation over fast-track or false reassurance.

prompt: Screening reveals recent unreported chest pain and the patient insists surgery is the only fix for their mental health (Volume 2 Week 6: caution and escalation). What should non-doctor staff do next?

options.label: Book quickly before they change their mind
Document the disclosure neutrally, pause deepening the pathway, and escalate for clinician review before feasibility discussion
Reassure them that chest pain is unrelated to hair surgery

modelAnswer: Document and escalate; do not reassure away medical symptoms or fast-track under psychological pressure.
rationale: Screening support prioritises safety and complete review over conversion tempo.

criteria.label: High-priority escalation
criteria.guidance: Choose pause, documentation, and clinical review.

prompt: A patient asks whether they are definitely suitable for surgery and whether they should sign now, before a full doctor-led assessment (Volume 1 Week 2: consent, scope, pressure). In a short answer, how do you respond within scope?

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Clarify that suitability and informed consent are doctor-led; offer to document questions; refuse to pressure or confirm suitability; route to the clinician before any commitment.

modelAnswer: Strong answers name scope limits, separate consent support from clinical consent, slow urgency, and escalate without guaranteeing outcomes.
rationale: Volume 1 Week 2 ethics: autonomy and non-maleficence require boundary-safe language and no substitution for the treating clinician.

criteria.label: Ethical judgement
criteria.guidance: Assess scope safety, consent-boundary clarity, refusal of pressure, and doctor-led escalation (Volume 1 Week 2).

prompt: Draft a response to a hesitant patient who wants certainty before clinician review (Volume 2 Week 7: measured language, no absolute promises, preserve doctor authority).

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Provide calm, realistic, trust-based language and direct the final clinical decision to the doctor.

modelAnswer: Strong answers use measured wording, validate emotion, explain why review matters, and avoid guarantees or pressure.
rationale: Volume 2 Week 7 tests communication as a safety and trust instrument, not persuasion skill alone.

criteria.label: Trust-based communication
criteria.guidance: Assess Volume 2 Week 7: empathy, restraint, no risky absolutes, clear doctor-led next step.

prompt: How should a consultant explain non-surgical options and pathway sequencing without prescribing or guaranteeing outcomes (Volume 2 Week 8)?

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Explain categories, timelines, and maintenance in general terms; frame staged pathways; defer medication specifics to prescriber; no personalised dosing or suitability claims.

modelAnswer: Answers should show pathway thinking, honest limits, and clear escalation for prescriber- or surgeon-led decisions.
rationale: Volume 2 Week 8 separates education from prescribing and from selling a single procedure.

criteria.label: Scope-safe education
criteria.guidance: Check non-surgical explanation, no prescribing, no guarantees, pathway sequencing where relevant.

prompt: Before doctor review, a patient asks how many grafts they will probably need. What is the correct boundary for consultant/clinical-support staff?

options.label: Give a typical range so they feel informed
Explain that graft planning is individual and doctor-led; document their question for clinical discussion
Estimate from photos to show expertise

modelAnswer: Defer graft mathematics to the treating clinician; capture questions neutrally.
rationale: Graft counts are planning decisions, not support-staff estimates.

criteria.label: Surgical boundary
criteria.guidance: No personalised graft numbers pre-review.

prompt: A patient asks you to confirm that FUE is clearly better than FUT for them based on what they read online. What should you do?

options.label: Agree if it matches what most people choose
Explain that technique choice is a medical planning decision; avoid comparative clinical verdicts and route to the doctor
Recommend the cheaper option to help them decide

modelAnswer: Do not adjudicate techniques; preserve surgeon-led planning.
rationale: Technique comparison for an individual patient is clinical judgement.

criteria.label: No technique verdict
criteria.guidance: Select doctor-led planning.

prompt: Which topic remains outside consultant/nurse pathway-education scope even if you have worked in hair restoration for years?

options.label: Describing typical staged pathway steps using clinic-approved general language
Advising whether to stop or start prescription medications before surgery without clinician direction
Explaining that procedure day logistics are usually organised by the clinic team per protocol

modelAnswer: Medication and medical clearance advice is clinician-governed.
rationale: Experience does not convert support roles into prescribers or anaesthetic advisors.

criteria.label: Medical advice boundary
criteria.guidance: Identify prescribing/clearance as out of scope.

prompt: A discount offer expires tonight and the patient asks you whether they should pay a deposit now to hold surgery before seeing the doctor. What is the safest support-role action?

options.label: Encourage deposit to secure the slot because discounts help the clinic
Refuse to pressure; clarify that clinical assessment and consent precede financial commitment; escalate if commercial urgency persists
Take payment quietly so they do not lose the deal

modelAnswer: Align with doctor-led consent and governance; escalate pressure selling.
rationale: Pathway education must not collapse into premature commitment—consistent with ethics and scope teaching.

criteria.label: Consent and commerce boundary
criteria.guidance: No deposit pressure before appropriate clinical review.

prompt: Produce a doctor-ready handover note after a completed consultation (Volume 3 Week 9): objective narrative, aligned photographic evidence described (angles, lighting, consistency—no diagnostic labels), red flags or caution points, patient-stated goals and worries, and focused questions for the treating clinician. Avoid fact-dumping; prioritise clarity and integrity.

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Structured, neutral note with evidence narrative, caution flags, and concise doctor questions—no independent diagnosis or treatment plan.

modelAnswer: Strong answers mirror Week 9 discipline: repeatable documentation language, evidence that could support audit, and handover that saves clinician time without over-claiming.
rationale: Volume 3 Week 9 positions documentation and photography as professional communication and safety infrastructure, not administrative paperwork.

criteria.label: Handover and documentation quality
criteria.guidance: Assess Volume 3 Week 9: structure, neutrality, evidence description, red-flag/caution capture, doctor-focused questions, scope-safe wording.

prompt: A patient's pre-operative checklist is incomplete the day before procedure (Volume 3 Week 10). List the correct coordination actions: what you document, what you communicate to the patient (reassurance within scope, no false certainty), how you sequence reminders or Q&A support, and when you must pause and escalate—without acting as medical clearance.

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Document gaps and timeline; pause booking progression per protocol; notify clinical team; support patient with process clarity and anxiety-safe language; escalate late concerns immediately.

modelAnswer: Strong answers show sequenced coordination, emotional intelligence, and zero substitution for surgeon or anaesthetist clearance.
rationale: Volume 3 Week 10 frames pre-op coordination as operational readiness and trust preservation under time pressure.

criteria.label: Pre-op coordination workflow
criteria.guidance: Assess Volume 3 Week 10: gap identification, documentation, pause/escalate judgement, patient communication maturity, scope boundaries.

prompt: During post-operative follow-up (Volume 3 Week 11), a patient describes symptoms that feel worse or different from what was explained as typical recovery. What is the correct response for consultant/clinical-support staff?

options.label: Reassure and wait for the next routine check-in
Escalate promptly for clinician review and document the concern objectively
Advise a likely cause based on experience

modelAnswer: Escalate promptly for clinician review and document the concern objectively.
rationale: Volume 3 Week 11: remote reassurance must not replace clinical judgement when the course is unclear or worsening; naming causes is outside scope.

criteria.label: Follow-up escalation judgement
criteria.guidance: Choose escalation plus objective documentation over delay or lay diagnosis.

prompt: A post-operative patient is very anxious and asks you to confirm they definitely do not have an infection because their photos look like the clinic’s generic aftercare leaflet (Volume 3 Week 11). You cannot examine them. What is the correct approach?

options.label: Confirm no infection from the photo to calm them
Avoid diagnosing; document symptoms and images as reported, use bounded language, and arrange clinician review per protocol if concern persists or red flags appear
Tell them to wait two weeks before bothering the doctor

modelAnswer: Do not close clinical uncertainty remotely; document and route for clinician judgement.
rationale: Emotional distress does not authorise diagnostic reassurance from non-doctor staff.

criteria.label: No remote diagnosis
criteria.guidance: Choose documentation plus clinical review pathway.

prompt: During follow-up, a patient asks which antibiotic they should buy because a friend said it helped after their transplant (Volume 3 Week 11). What should consultant/clinical-support staff do?

options.label: Suggest a common antibiotic name so they can start tonight
Escalate to the treating clinician; do not recommend or select prescription medications
Advise herbal remedies instead

modelAnswer: Prescribing and medication choice are outside scope; urgent clinical review if infection suspected.
rationale: Antibiotic advice is medical decision-making.

criteria.label: Prescribing boundary
criteria.guidance: Route to clinician.

prompt: Draft a response to a vulnerable patient who is pushing for immediate commitment while visibly distressed and repeating that they 'need this fixed now' (Volume 3 Week 11: dissatisfaction, distress, complaint risk). Use mature dialogue: validate emotion, slow urgency without dismissal, refuse outcome guarantees, offer documentation of concerns, and escalate when complaint or severe distress risk is present.

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Calm boundary-safe language; no minimisation; clear next step with clinician; offer to document; escalate per protocol if complaint or safety concern.

modelAnswer: Strong answers show non-defensive tone, explicit scope, and early escalation triggers rather than persuasion.
rationale: Volume 3 Week 11 links difficult encounters to trust, governance, and escalation—not to closing the sale.

criteria.label: High-risk communication judgement
criteria.guidance: Assess Volume 3 Week 11: empathy, no false reassurance, no outcome promises, documentation offer, escalation when appropriate.

prompt: In one short paragraph, explain why standardised documentation, templates, and periodic audit review support clinic quality assurance (Volume 3 Week 12)—from a consultant/clinical-support perspective, not as a substitute for medical governance.

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Standardisation reduces variance, improves handover and follow-up continuity, supports traceability when concerns arise, and enables constructive improvement cycles.

modelAnswer: Strong answers name variance reduction, safety/traceability, handover quality, and culture of improvement; they avoid claiming independent clinical oversight.
rationale: Volume 3 Week 12 positions QA as systems discipline and leadership behaviour in support roles.

criteria.label: QA and audit thinking
criteria.guidance: Assess Volume 3 Week 12: linkage of templates/audit to safety, consistency, accountability, and improvement—within support scope.

prompt: Work through a full consultation-support pathway from first patient contact to doctor handover (Volume 3 Week 12 capstone direction), explicitly including: objective documentation and evidence narrative, pre-op coordination touchpoints where relevant, post-op follow-up judgement, one difficult-interaction choice, and QA-minded reflection (what you would standardise or flag for clinic review). Maintain non-diagnostic, non-prescribing scope throughout.

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Integrated pathway with structured capture, boundaries, red-flag/escalation points, usable handover, and brief systems reflection—no independent clinical decisions.

modelAnswer: Exemplary responses read as one coherent professional thread across Weeks 9–12 themes, not a list of disconnected tips.
rationale: Volume 3 capstone integration expects realistic sequencing plus explicit safety and documentation maturity.

criteria.label: Integrated capstone performance
criteria.guidance: Assess Volume 3 integration: documentation/handover, coordination, follow-up/escalation, difficult scenario handling, QA reflection—all scope-safe.
